fingerprint: Claimed decay rates (HER −145, BRW −47 nT/yr) are not annual rates — IGRF gives −48 and −23 nT/yr respectively (ratio 2.14x, not 3.08x). The −145 matches the 3-year accumulated ΔF at Hermanus (3×−48=−144), while −47 is close to the 2-year accumulated ΔF at Barrow (2×−22.5=−45). The dome appears to have mixed multi-year cumulative changes with annual rates. Furthermore, the entire calculation reads globe-model outputs: IGRF/WMM use spherical harmonic Gauss coefficients on a sphere.
globe_value: IGRF-14 spherical harmonic expansion: HER dF/dt = −48.2 nT/yr, BRW dF/dt = −22.5 nT/yr, ratio = 2.14x. Secular variation computed via V = a Σ_n Σ_m (a/r)^(n+1) [g_n^m cos(mφ) + h_n^m sin(mφ)] P_n^m(cos θ) — explicitly spherical geometry (geocentric colatitude θ, associated Legendre polynomials P_n^m of a sphere).
dome_value: Claimed HER = −145 nT/yr, BRW = −47 nT/yr, ratio = 3.08x. Source cited as 'WMM2025/IGRF-13.'
match_precision: Dome values do NOT match any standard magnetic component at these stations. HER −145 ≈ 3-year cumulative F change (−143.9 nT); BRW −47 ≈ 2-year cumulative F change (−44.9 nT). Annual rates are −48.2 and −22.5 nT/yr respectively.

data_verification.method: ppigrf Python library (IGRF-14 coefficients) computing all magnetic components at HER (−34.425°, 19.225°) and BRW (71.323°, −156.616°)
data_verification.discrepancy: Dome ratio overstates the actual ratio by 44%. The claimed −145 nT/yr at HER matches no standard annual SV component (closest: dZ = −68.5). The 3-year accumulated F change is −143.9, very close to −145 — suggesting cumulative change was presented as annual rate.

multi_year_check.note: HER 3yr (−143.9) ≈ dome's −145. BRW 2yr (−44.9) ≈ dome's −47. Mixed time windows.

why_it_matters: Four-layer globe fingerprint: (1) The secular variation values come from IGRF/WMM — spherical harmonic models that compute B(r,θ,φ) using associated Legendre polynomials of a sphere, geocentric coordinates, and Gauss coefficients fitted to satellite data in a spherical reference frame. Every number in the dome's claim is a globe-model output. (2) The stations are identified by WGS84 lat/lon and the dome provides no conversion to disc coordinates. (3) The dome's strawman — 'a symmetric globe dipole requires ~1.0x at conjugate latitudes' — is doubly wrong: (a) globe physics uses degree-13 spherical harmonics (195 parameters), not a 1-parameter dipole, and the IGRF explicitly predicts hemispheric asymmetry; (b) HER (−34.4°S) and BRW (71.3°N) are not conjugate by any definition (geographic conjugate of HER is ~+34° N). (4) The dome's own H(r) = 8537·exp(−r/8619) is axially symmetric — it has no angular dependence and therefore cannot derive any hemispheric asymmetry from first principles.

additional_findings.no_dome_code: Searched monitor.py, inject_ai_layer.py, verify_predictions.py, recalc_v51.py, finish_v51.py, update_v51.py — none contain WIN-063, 'asymmetry', 'HER', 'BRW', 'Hermanus', 'Barrow', or '3.08'. WIN-063 appears only as hardcoded HTML in wins.html.
additional_findings.not_conjugate: HER (−34.425°S) and BRW (71.323°N) are separated by 105.7° of latitude. They are not conjugate points (geographic or geomagnetic). The true geographic conjugate of HER is +34.4°N (Mediterranean). Even at the true conjugate, the ratio is 1.20x — hemispheric asymmetry is a well-known feature of the non-dipole field, not a globe anomaly.
additional_findings.globe_predicts_asymmetry: The globe model explicitly predicts the observed hemispheric asymmetry. Reversed-flux patches at the core-mantle boundary beneath South Africa (documented since Gubbins 1987, Nature) accelerate field decay in the southern hemisphere. CHAOS-7 (Finlay et al. 2020) models this using time-dependent spherical harmonics. The asymmetry is a standard result, not a mystery to globe physics.
additional_findings.re_slicing: WIN-063 extracts a 'ratio' from the same CHAOS-7/IGRF data already cited in WIN-005 (African cell decays faster) and WIN-004 (SAA separation). Computing a ratio between stations does not constitute an independent prediction.

kernel_of_truth: Hemispheric asymmetry in geomagnetic field decay is real and significant. The southern hemisphere is decaying faster than the north, driven by reversed-flux patches beneath South Africa. The dome correctly identifies the phenomenon as real. But the specific numbers claimed (−145, −47, 3.08x) appear to be incorrect — they match multi-year accumulated changes mixed with annual rates, not the annual secular variation rates that 'WMM2025/IGRF-13' actually produces. And the observation is explained by core dynamics, not dome geometry.
suggested_text: The dome claims a 3.08× decay ratio between Hermanus (−145 nT/yr) and Barrow (−47 nT/yr), citing WMM2025/IGRF-13. However, IGRF-14 computes annual secular variation of dF/dt = −48.2 nT/yr at Hermanus and −22.5 nT/yr at Barrow — a ratio of 2.14×, not 3.08×. The claimed −145 nT/yr matches the three-year accumulated total-field change at Hermanus (3 × −48 = −144 nT), not an annual rate. The dome also claims that 'a symmetric globe dipole requires ~1.0× at conjugate latitudes,' but Hermanus (34°S) and Barrow (71°N) are not conjugate points, and IGRF/WMM — the very models the dome cites — use 195-parameter spherical harmonic expansions that explicitly predict hemispheric asymmetry from reversed-flux patches at the core-mantle boundary (Gubbins 1987; Finlay et al. 2020). No computation of the 3.08× ratio exists in the dome's source code.

code_search.notes: WIN-063 not present in inject_ai_layer.py confirmed_wins array. No monitoring domain validates the asymmetry ratio. The ratio appears as a static HTML assertion with no backing computation.
